title: Antibacterial mechanism of CO<sub>2</sub> combined with low temperature against Shewanella putrefaciens by biochemical and metabolomics analysis.
author_string: Li, Peiyun, Mei, Jun, Xie, Jing
abstract: To further reveal the inhibition mechanism of carbon dioxide (CO<sub>2</sub>) on Shewanella putrefaciens (S. putrefaciens), influence on metabolic function was studied by biochemical and metabolomics analysis. Accordingly, reduction of intracellular pH (pH<sub>i</sub>), depolarization of cell membrane and accumulation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) indicated that CO<sub>2</sub> changed the membrane permeability of S. putrefaciens. Besides, adenosine triphosphate (ATP), ATPase, nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NAD<sup>+</sup>/NADH) and ratios of NADH/NAD<sup>+</sup> were detected, indicating a role of CO<sub>2</sub> in repressing respiratory pathway and electron transport. According to metabolomics results, CO<sub>2</sub> induced differential expressions of metabolites, disordered respiratory chain and weakened energy metabolism of S. putrefaciens. Inhibition of respiratory rate-limiting enzymes also revealed that electron transfer of respiratory chain was blocked, cell respiration was weakened, and thus energy supply was insufficient under CO<sub>2</sub> stress. These results revealed that CO<sub>2</sub> caused disruption of metabolic function, which might be the main cause of growth inhibition for S. putrefaciens.
